when you're the one who built them.
The kettle hums,
but it's not the water boiling—
It's the quiet click
when everything fits.
Let them know.

scripts.topic: The Voice in Your Head That Says You Are Not Enough
scripts.script:
How do you name the guest
who never knocks
but unpacks its bags in your bones,
quiet and persistent,
not asking,
just rearranging the furniture of your ribs
until every breath feels borrowed?
Some voices don’t shout—
they hum lullabies of doubt,
soft enough to mistake for your own.
The hands that built you
keep measuring the cracks,
but the cracks were always doors.
Let them open.

scripts.topic: When You Cry in the Car Alone
scripts.script:
How do you name the sound
when tears hit the steering wheel?
It’s not crying,
just the engine of sadness
running on empty.
The radio hums a song you once loved,
but the chorus skips
like a heart missing its exit.
Somewhere between the rearview and the road,
you become a passenger
in your own body.
The parking brake stays up,
but the car rolls anyway.
Let them think you’re stuck in traffic.

scripts.topic: You Make the Hard Days Easier
scripts.script:
I stopped counting the cracks in the ceiling.
Your voice turns them into constellations,
soft as the hum of a fridge at 3 a.m.
No one notices how you rearrange the air,
but I do.
The way you exhale becomes my gravity—
pulling the weight of spreadsheets and unanswered texts
down to a manageable hum.
I never told you how your laugh
sounds like a zipper closing on a winter coat,
snug and sudden.
The hard days don’t vanish.
They just learn to share the bed.

Your side of the bed stays cold.
Not from winter,
but the way you stopped apologizing for breathing.

I trace the cracks in the ceiling,
counting the hours you saved yourself.
No grand speeches,
just the quiet click of a door closing on guilt.

The mirror doesn’t flinch anymore.
Neither do you.

Send this to them.

scripts.topic: How to Carry Loss Without Being Crushed
scripts.script:
You’re folding the laundry again.
The sleeves still smell like rain on your skin.
One sock remains unpaired,
but you tuck it in the drawer anyway.
LOSS ISN’T A STONE YOU CARRY,
it’s the quiet way the drawer sticks,
just enough to remind you it’s there.
You press harder,
and the wood gives,
slightly,
like a held breath.
The house exhales around you.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: When You Delete the Message Before Sending It
scripts.script:
I stopped the cursor mid-air,
hovering like a held breath.
The screen glows,
just enough to see my doubt,
but not enough to read it aloud.
Some words are too heavy
for the space between us,
so I fold them,
press them into my ribs,
and let the delete key whisper
what I never could.
send this to them.

They’re not unsent. Just unfinished.
The cursor blinks like a metronome,
keeping time for a song you’ll never sing.
Your thumb hovers,
but the words dissolve like sugar on your tongue.
SOME THOUGHTS ARE TOO SWEET TO SHARE.
They ferment in the dark of your drafts,
becoming something richer,
something only you can taste.
The screen dims,
and you let it.
Send this to them.

Hafiz knew
what the body remembers
before the mind does.

it’s the scent of rain
on wool before the storm breaks,
the way a doorframe holds
your shoulder when you lean,
the quiet hum of a fridge
that’s always been there.

not the walls, not the roof,
but the space between things
where your breath slows
and the world tilts just enough
to let you rest.

you’ll forget the address,
but the feeling stays.

scripts.topic: When Just Being Near You Is Enough
scripts.script:
You don’t need to speak.
Not at all.
The air between us
carries more than words,
just…
a quiet hum of knowing.
Some nights,
I trace the shape of your breathing
like a constellation I’ve memorized.
Not to name it,
just to feel it there,
steady and warm.
You don’t have to do anything.
I’m already home.
visual_keywords: unmade bed with one pillow indented

scripts.script:
I stopped tightening the jar lids,
just to prove I could.
Some things open easier
when you let them breathe.
The stubborn rust,
the salt that clings,
they soften
in their own time.
I won’t crack the shell
just to see the yolk run.
Some gifts arrive
already peeled,
quiet and whole.

How do you love someone
without dissolving into them,
like salt in water?
The ocean doesn’t ask the waves
to stop being waves,
just to remember
they were always part of something bigger.
Some hands hold too gently,
and some hold too tight,
but the ones that stay
are the ones that learn
the shape of your edges.
You don’t have to lose yourself
to prove you’re found.

You're tracing circles on my wrist.
A compass with no north.
Every loop,
just a promise,
soft as the hum of a fridge at night.
I could map your touch
by the way my pulse slows,
like a train arriving on time.
No grand speeches,
only the quiet click of a seatbelt.
You're the kind of steady
that makes leaving feel like misplacing something.
Stay here.
Send this to them.

It wasn’t love. Not the kind you name.
The first time I knew,
you were a tide
pulling me under
without a sound.
Not drowning,
just… disappearing
into the shape of your hands.
I let the current take me,
not because I trusted it,
but because I forgot how to swim.
You never asked for rescue.
Neither did I.

Lao Tzu knew
water doesn’t rush the stone
it simply waits
carves the deepest canyons
without a sound.

you think stillness is weakness
but watch the river bend
around every rock
you’ve ever carried

it doesn’t fight
yet nothing resists it

Some friendships are like subway tokens,
kept in a drawer long past the last ride.
They jingle,
reminding you of turnstiles,
of shared transfers,
of destinations you no longer visit.
The metal warms to your touch,
but the gates stay shut.
You still check your pockets,
just in case,
but the trains have moved on.

Grief isn’t a storm,
just a river rerouting.
I trace the new banks,
my fingers along cold stone,
learning the shape of absence.
Some mornings,
the water runs backward,
carrying yesterday’s fish,
shimmering,
then gone.
I don’t ask why,
just kneel,
and let the current teach me.

scripts.topic: You Didn't Fix Me, You Stayed
scripts.script:
The thermostat was always set to your number.
I shivered anyway,
not from cold,
but the way you never asked why.
Some nights,
the house hums like a held breath,
and I realize—
you didn’t patch the cracks,
you just learned to dance in the draft.
I keep the windows open,
not for air,
but for the sound of you staying.

I LOCK the door
softly
so no one hears the click
then I sit on the floor
hug my knees
and let the corners of my mouth
drop like forgotten grocery bags
spilling milk and eggs
onto linoleum
YOU WOULD NEVER KNOW
I just needed a minute
to stop pretending
to be happy.

How do you explain to someone
that their favorite sweater
no longer fits you?
The sleeves hang past your wrists,
the collar sags like a sigh,
but the stitches still remember your name.
You don’t rip it,
don’t toss it in the giveaway bin—
just fold it neatly,
place it on the top shelf,
where the dust gathers
and the memories stay soft.
Let them keep their shape.

The last text you sent still says "delivered."
Not read,
just floating
like a boat with no anchor.
You check it less now,
but the habit lingers—
The same fingers that scrolled for their name
slowly learn to trace your own.
Some days,
you wake up
and the bed feels like a shore,
not a shipwreck.
You belong here,
quiet and unshaken.
Let the tide come.

scripts.topic: The Weight of Being the Strong One
scripts.script:
There’s a quiet that isn’t peace,
not the absence of sound,
but the weight of a bridge
built to hold everyone else.
The beams don’t creak,
they just bow,
silent under the traffic of need.
Some shoulders remember the shape of hands,
but forget how to ask for help.
I won’t collapse,
just…
sink a little deeper into the earth,
carrying what was never mine to keep.

You’re fine. Of course you’re fine.
The coffee’s gone cold in your hands,
but you keep stirring anyway.
Some mornings,
the spoon scrapes louder than your thoughts.
You laugh,
just a little too bright,
and the room tilts
like a ship losing balance.
No one notices
the way your smile
folds into itself,
a paper crane too fragile to fly.
You’re fine.
The words taste like salt,
but you swallow them whole.
visual_keywords: shadows stretching across a kitchen table

scripts.topic: The Difference Between Living and Existing
scripts.script:
Living is a room you paint yourself.
The walls remember every brushstroke,
but the ceiling,
that blank canvas,
stares back,
asking what you’ll dare to dream.
Existing is just the floor,
holding you up,
while you count the cracks,
not the colors.
Some people never pick up the brush.
They call it safety.
I call it a life half-lived.
Stay where the air smells like possibility.

scripts.topic: Being Strong for Everyone While Breaking Inside
scripts.script:
You're holding the steering wheel too tight.
Your knuckles are bone-white moons,
but the road hums like a lullaby.
EVERYONE in the backseat is asleep.
You swallow the crack in your voice,
pretend it's just a yawn.
The dashboard glows,
soft as a confession no one hears.
Your ribs ache,
not from the drive,
from the weight of being the only one awake.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: When You Realized You Were the Option, Not the Priority
scripts.script:
Hafiz whispered
somewhere between your name
and the door closing,
you became
a question mark
not an answer.

I kept the text unread
like a train ticket
to a city that left without me.
The platform empty,
the schedule rewritten.
You were never late.
I was just early
to my own absence.

Hafiz whispered
when you found me
I was a bowl of salt
left in the rain.

not melted
not dissolved
just softer at the edges
where the water touched.

I didn’t know I could hold
so much without breaking
or that kindness
could taste like the sea.

now I collect the drops
that fall from your hands.

scripts.topic: The Beauty of an Unfinished Life
scripts.script:
You’re tracing the crack in your coffee mug.
It splits like a river on your palm,
but the clay still holds.
WE ALL CARRY fractures,
only some choose to drink from them.
Your life isn’t a story,
it’s a sketchbook—
pages half-filled with charcoal,
smudged edges where the eraser gave up.
AND THAT’S THE BEAUTY.
The unfinished lines hum,
a quiet current beneath your ribs.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Way You Look at Me
scripts.script:
YOUR GAZE IS A ROOM
I never knew
I could fit inside.
Every time you turn,
the walls breathe,
soft as a sweater pulled over my head.
I forget my name
till you say it,
and the air hums
like a radio tuned to a station
that only plays us.

There's a kind of ache that wears lipstick,
not for beauty,
but for armor.
The same mouth that laughs at jokes
slowly forgets how to speak,
just hums the melody of okay.
Some smiles are just mirrors,
reflecting what you need to see,
not the cracks underneath.
I won't break the glass,
quietly polishing the surface,
until the reflection shatters.
send this to them.

Hafiz whispered
like a tide returning
to the same shore
you drift back
to their name.

it’s not the heart that lingers
it’s the habit of the moon
pulling at the waves
long after the ship has sailed.

you don’t want them
just the ghost of wanting
clinging to your ribs
like salt on old wood.

you’ll let go
when the shore forgets the sea.

scripts.topic: How to Carry Loss Without Being Crushed
scripts.script:
You learn to hold it
like a snowflake
on your tongue
before it melts—
not to keep
but to know
it was real.
The weight doesn’t vanish
it just
settles differently
like dust
on a windowsill
no one wipes.

YOU KNOW THE WAY
back to yourself
when the clock hands melt
into syrup
slow enough to lick
YOU FORGET THE RULES
you wrote
in invisible ink
on your ribs
and suddenly
the floor is lava
but you
are already dancing

You left your jacket on my chair
again
I didn’t ask for it back
but I wore it to bed
smelling like your cologne
and the diner where we split fries
I kept the sleeves too long
folded them like secrets
YOU NEVER NOTICED
I stopped wearing my own name
till it sounded borrowed
like a key that fits
a lock you’ve already moved from

I stopped counting the cracks in the ceiling.
Your name fits perfectly in every one.
The sheets still hum your favorite song,
but the melody skips on my ribs now.
I trace the shape of your absence,
like a child learning cursive in spilled milk.
The fridge hums,
the clock chews its own teeth,
and I pretend this is enough.
Some nights,
the quiet isn’t empty.
It’s just waiting for you to text back.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: What I'd Tell You If I Could
scripts.script:
If I could,
I’d whisper through the cracks in the pavement,
where rain collects like secrets.
Some days,
the sidewalk remembers every step you took,
but forgets why.
I’d trace the lines,
not to erase,
just to say,
I’m still here,
watching the puddles reflect,
what we almost were,
but never named.

You held the door
for the storm to pass
and it did.

NOTHING moved
not the clock
not your pulse
not the dust in the corner.

You learned
how to be a room
that doesn’t need to speak
to be full.

scripts.topic: Falling For Someone Who Does Not Know
scripts.script:
You leave crumbs
on the counter
I pretend not to notice
but my hands
linger
where yours just were.
EVERY MORNING
I rehearse your name
like a prayer
no one’s listening to.
The kettle hums
and I wonder
if you’d like this tea
if you’d stay
for the second cup.

You're stirring honey into tea again.
The spoon clinks like a tiny bell.
Each slow circle
pulls the sweetness from the bottom,
but the ache stays suspended.
IT'S NOT THAT IT HURTS LESS.
It's that the hurt becomes the shape of your cup.
You drink it anyway.
The first sip burns,
then your throat remembers how to hold fire.
This is how you learn to carry yourself.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: When You Are the One Who Always Forgives
scripts.script:
You always fold the note
in half
one more time
than it needs
so the ink won’t bleed through
onto your palm.
I KNOW
by the way you tuck it
into your sleeve
like a secret
not meant for me.
WE WALK
and the pavement remembers
every step you took back
while I stood still.
Still
holding the shape of you.

I KNEW the river would bend
if I waited
if I sat
on the bank with my shoes off
and let the current
carry the stones I no longer needed
ONE by one
past my toes
until the water ran clear
and the ache
was just the shape of my feet
in the mud.

Hafiz whispered
pain hides
like salt in bread—
you taste nothing
until the crust cracks.

you’ve learned to smile
with a mouth full of it,
swallow the burn
without a cough.

some days the body remembers
what the mind buried—
a single tear
no one asked for.

The voicemail still saves your last laugh.
It plays when the apartment hums too quiet,
like a secret I keep feeding.
I trace the shape of your name
in the steam on my bathroom mirror,
but the letters blur before I finish.
Some nights I swear I hear your keys
jingle in the hallway,
and my breath catches mid-sigh.
The couch still dips where you sat,
waiting for me to come home.
I leave it empty,
just in case.

The clock hands froze,
midair,
ticking backward in your mind.
You didn’t notice,
just let the numbers blur,
like rain on a train window.
Somewhere,
a suitcase lost its wheels,
rolled into a corner,
and you stopped waiting for the station.
The ticket in your pocket,
softened,
like old paper in the rain.

I stopped counting the cracks in the sidewalk.
Every step forward a small surrender.
The pavement remembers my hesitation,
but the rain washes the old routes clean.
I trace new lines with my toes,
soft as a child learning to write.
The city hums,
oblivious to the courage it takes.
I press my palm to the nearest wall,
feel the pulse of something beginning.
This time,
the story starts with me.

You’re still folding the napkin.
The crease already perfect,
yet your fingers hesitate.
A table set for two,
now just crumbs and a single cup.
The steam from your tea
curls like a question mark.
You trace the rim,
counting the seconds
you promised yourself you’d leave.
But the sugar bowl is empty,
and you keep refilling it.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: You Make the Hard Days Easier
scripts.script:
You’re stirring honey into tea.
The spoon clinks too loud.
I watch your wrist,
the way it circles,
patient as a tide learning sand.
Some days,
the weight of everything
presses like a thumb on my sternum.
You don’t ask why.
You just slide the mug closer,
the steam curling between us,
thick as a promise.
Drink.
I do.

The river doesn’t apologize,
for the shape of its banks.
It bends,
just to keep moving.
Some mornings,
the current carries secrets,
not to hide them,
but to let them breathe.
I stopped fighting the flow,
and the water remembered my name.
Now,
even the stones listen.

scripts.topic: The Meaning We Choose to Find
scripts.script:
The last voicemail plays on shuffle.
It skips,
like a heart missing a beat.
I don’t delete it,
not because I’m sentimental,
but because some meanings
are too heavy to bury.
The static hums your name,
and I let it,
like a prayer I no longer believe in.
We were never the story,
just the footnote someone else wrote.
Now I choose the ink.

scripts.topic: What Truly Matters in the End
scripts.script:
There's a quiet like the last page of a book
no one finishes,
just the spine still warm from your fingers.
Some moments
are just the sound of your own breath
in a room you thought was empty.
The clock ticks,
but the hands move backward
when you're not looking.
I won't tell you what to keep,
just...
let the small things
be the ones that stay.
let them know.

Lying awake replays every word like a skipping record.
The needle won’t lift,
just circles the same scratch
over and over.
Some nights,
the groove wears so deep
you can’t hear the song anymore,
only the static of what you should’ve said.
The silence between thoughts
holds more weight than the bed,
and the ceiling cracks
like old promises.
Let them sleep.

scripts.topic: The Voice in Your Head That Says You Are Not Enough
scripts.script:
It’s the needle in your ribs,
steady,
pricking every breath.
Some days it hums like a fridge,
quiet,
just loud enough to remind you.
The voice doesn’t shout,
just taps,
like a finger on glass.
You try to ignore it,
but the tap,
becomes a drip,
becomes the whole damn sink.
And suddenly,
you’re counting cracks in the ceiling,
wondering if they’re counting you.

You're tracing circles on the tabletop.
The sugar grains stick to your fingertip,
like the way your laugh used to cling to my ribs.
I didn’t know how empty the room was
until you filled it with the sound of your keys.
The kettle hums,
but it’s not the same pitch.
I measure time in spoons now—
how many it takes to stir memory into coffee.
You left the salt open,
and suddenly everything tastes like you.
I don’t close it.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: What You Cannot Change and What You Can
scripts.script:
Friday night. The clock hums.
You trace the cracks in the ceiling,
counting what’s already decided—
the rent,
the name on the lease,
the way your coffee goes cold.
But your hands,
still warm,
can turn the page,
choose the next song,
whisper the first line of something new.
The walls stay standing,
but the story inside
is yours to rewrite.

scripts.topic: When You Love Someone Who Is Leaving
scripts.script:
You don’t leave. You just evaporate.
The kettle hums the same note,
but the steam carries your shape away.
I trace the air where your laugh used to sit,
now just a ghost of Earl Grey.
The mug still warms my palms,
but the heat is borrowed.
I drink the quiet,
swallow the absence like sugar.
The kitchen clock ticks,
counting the seconds you’re not here.
I’ll pour the rest down the drain.
send this to them.

I carry the scent of your laundry detergent everywhere.
Every time I fold a shirt,
the memory of your hands lingers.
Soft,
like the way you tuck the blanket around my feet.
I never told you how much I notice.
The way you stir your coffee,
clockwise,
three slow circles before you sip.
And the quiet click of your keyboard,
a lullaby I’ve memorized.
Some nights I trace the pattern,
pretending it’s your fingers on my skin.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: When You Make Ordinary Moments Feel Different
scripts.script:
You're stirring sugar into tea
like it's the first time
The spoon clinks
but the sound is softer
not because the cup changed
but because you did
Some moments taste different
when you're the one holding the cup
The steam rises
and for once
you don't look away
let it warm your face
this small thing matters

There's a tenderness that lingers
like the last note of a lullaby.
Your fingers trace circles on my wrist,
unhurried,
and the world shrinks to this.
A single breath between us
becomes a language all its own.
I memorize the slope of your shoulder,
the way it cradles moonlight
when you think no one's watching.
These small things,
they hold the shape of us.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: When Love Feels Like Coming Home
scripts.script:
You don’t belong here. Not like this.
The kitchen hums the same old tune,
but the melody’s softer now.
Like a spoon stirring honey,
you dissolve into the warmth.
No questions,
just the quiet click of the kettle.
I forget where I end,
and the room begins.
Your laughter,
a familiar crease in the air.
This is how walls remember.
Send this to them.

You’re smiling again.
The same corners,
the same practiced lift,
but your teeth are holding back a scream.
Somewhere inside,
a clockwork heart winds down—
tick,
tick,
just one more hour.
The hands that cup your face
are learning to let go,
and the mirror
only shows the cracks you’ve glued shut.
You laugh,
but the sound is a door
slowly closing.
Let them think it’s real.

scripts.topic: The Small Things I Notice About You
scripts.script:
The way your fingers trace,
the rim of a coffee cup,
like you’re reading braille in steam.
Every morning,
I memorize the pattern.
Some days it’s slow,
other days it’s a stutter,
but always,
you leave half-moons in the porcelain.
I count them,
like constellations no one else sees.
They’re my quiet almanac,
predicting the weather of your thoughts.

scripts.topic: Friends Who Only Call When They Need You
scripts.script:
THEY TEXT AT 2 AM
like clockwork
like rent’s due
like you’re the only one
who remembers how to fix things.

You answer
because the phone still glows
with their name
and for a second
you forget
it’s never the other way around.

They ask,
"Hey, you up?"
and you lie,
"Yeah."

scripts.topic: What I Never Said Out Loud
scripts.script:
The clock still ticks after goodbyes.
Not louder,
just emptier.
I counted seconds,
not to forget,
but to remember
how long it took to breathe again.
Some words
stay tangled in my ribs,
soft as a whisper,
sharp as a held breath.
I never said them out loud.
The room still knows.

You scroll past their name again.
Not with anger,
just a quiet exhale.
The suitcase you packed years ago
still sits half-open,
but the clothes inside don’t fit anymore.
Some people are seasons,
bright and necessary,
then suddenly,
you’re wearing a coat in July.
Leave the door unlocked,
but don’t wait up.

The hands that once begged,
now build.
Not bricks,
but boundaries,
soft as morning dew,
but unshakable.
Some people call it cold,
I call it breathing.
The room is smaller now,
and yet,
it fits me perfectly.
No more folding,
just standing,
taller than the asking.

scripts.topic: The Way You Look When You Do Not Know I Am Watching
scripts.script:
You’re not hiding.
Not really.
just folding the edges of yourself
like a letter you’ll never send,
soft creases where the words should be.
The way your fingers trace the rim of a cup
as if it’s the only thing holding you here,
not me,
not the room,
just porcelain and steam.
I could watch forever,
but some secrets aren’t mine to keep.
let them stay.

The river doesn’t fight the stone,
just wears it smooth,
patient as the tide.
Some days,
strength isn’t a roar,
but the quiet hum of water
carving canyons without sound.
The hands that hold the wheel steady,
not because they’re unshaken,
but because they know the storm passes,
and stillness is the compass.

scripts.topic: When You Miss Someone Who Is Still Alive
scripts.script:
It’s not absence. It’s a room
no one left
but the door won’t close.
I press my palm
against the hinge
and feel the draft
you used to walk through.
Some nights
the hallway hums
with the shape of your laugh
just out of reach.
I don’t call.
I let the echo warm me.

How do you name the space
where hope used to live?
The room feels smaller now,
not emptied,
just rearranged.
The chairs still face the door,
but no one’s coming.
Some nights,
you catch yourself listening
for footsteps that stopped years ago.
The key you kept on the hook
slowly gathers dust,
and you don’t move it.
It’s not sadness.
It’s the quiet click
of a door locking itself.

scripts.topic: The One I Think About First
scripts.script:
I still trace the shape of your name
on fogged glass
when the shower runs too long.
It steams away,
but my fingers remember
the curve of every letter.
Some habits don’t need practice,
they just live in your bones.
I won’t scrub it off,
even when the mirror clears.
You’re the first thought
that arrives without knocking.
Let them see.

The first time was an accident.
I tripped,
and there you were
catching me mid-air.
Not like a net,
like a current—
I didn’t fight it.
Some bodies remember kindness
even when the mind forgets.
Your hands still know
the shape of my falling,
quiet as a held breath.
I let you stay.

scripts.topic: When No One Asks If You Are Okay
scripts.script:
You’re stirring honey into tea
no one will taste.
The spoon scrapes porcelain,
steady,
just to hear something break the quiet.
Some mornings,
your reflection blinks first.
You laugh,
but the sound doesn’t travel past your collarbone.
The air in the room
thickens with questions you won’t ask.
You swallow them whole,
like sugar dissolving on the tongue
unnoticed.

You’re unfolding the blanket again
just to check the stitches
52 times you’ve done this
not counting
The way it still holds your shape
soft as the first night you slept here
Some places remember you
like a favorite chair remembers the weight of a body
not asking
not leaving
just… waiting
You don’t have to explain the tears
The blanket already knows
how to catch them.

The kettle hums,
low and forgotten,
steam curling like old promises.
Some nights,
the tea goes cold
before the first sip,
leaving only the ghost of warmth.
I traced your name
in the condensation,
watched it fade
without a sound.
Love was the cup,
but never the drink.

The thermostat still knows your side of the bed.
It clicks on at 2:17,
just like your breathing used to.
I never reset the schedule.
Every morning the house learns you again,
warms the air where your shoulder would press.
The radiator hums your name,
soft as the way you’d say mine after waking.
Now the sheets remember nothing,
but the walls keep the temperature.
I leave it running.
Send this to them.

Trust isn’t a bridge. It’s the river.
You step in,
and the current decides if you float.
Some days,
the water holds you like a promise written in braille.
Other days,
it drags you under,
but the rocks are smooth now,
worn by every fall.
You don’t drown.
You learn to read the ripples.
The river doesn’t ask for faith.
It just keeps moving.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: When You Miss Who They Used to Be
scripts.script:
I keep their old laugh
in my pocket
like a subway token
no turnstile accepts anymore.
The way they’d tilt their head
just so
when the joke landed—
GONE.
Now they answer questions
with the precision
of a metronome.
I trace the cracks
on the sidewalk
where we used to trip
over nothing.

I stopped counting the cracks in my teacup.
Every chip holds a morning I thought I’d break.
The porcelain remembers what my hands refuse.
Steam rises like a question no one asks.
I trace the rim,
wondering if the heat ever changes the clay.
Some burns don’t scar,
they soften the edges.
The cup still holds tea,
still spills,
still knows how to warm.
Send this to them.

I stopped counting the ways your laughter fits
into my pockets like loose change.
Every time I reach,
the clink reminds me
you’re still here.
The houseplant on your windowsill
thrives on the humidity of my sighs,
and I water it
with the words I never say.
Your name is a sweater I wear
under everything,
soft and slightly too big.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Way You Look at Me
scripts.script:
The way you look at me,
isn’t seeing—
it’s a slow unraveling.
Every glance,
a seam pulled loose,
until the stitches whisper.
Some moments,
time forgets to tick,
just hums in the space between us.
I don’t need to be found,
just held in the quiet pull
of your attention,
like a compass needle
pointing north to your skin.
visual_keywords: a compass spinning on a wooden table

scripts.topic: When Healing Hurts More Than the Wound
scripts.script:
THE SCRUBBING BRUSH
stings,
hot water
scalds,
but you keep going—
peeling back layers
like old wallpaper,
strips of yourself
curling at your feet.
YOU TELL ME
it’s supposed to hurt less,
but every rinse
reopens the cut.
The soap bubbles
pop,
and the ache
stays.

scripts.topic: What Truly Matters in the End
scripts.script:
You're folding the same shirt again.
Third time tonight.
The collar still won't sit right,
but...
your fingers remember
how his hands adjusted it for you.
Some nights,
that's all that lingers—
the way someone
once fixed what was crooked
without you asking.
Let them know.

scripts.topic: When You Love Someone Who Is Leaving
scripts.script:
The last text still glows on your screen.
I watch your thumb hesitate,
then save my name under "Home."
NOTHING FEELS LIKE IT USED TO
but the way your laugh lingers in the hallway,
soft as the scent of rain on wool.
You leave your favorite mug behind,
chipped at the rim where your lip always touched.
I trace the crack with my finger,
pretending it’s your skin I’m memorizing.
The train ticket’s in your pocket now,
folded like a secret you don’t want to keep.
I’ll let you go,
but first—let me steal this quiet moment.

There’s a kind of ache that lives in drafts,
not in the sending,
just in the hovering,
fingers trembling above glass,
like a held breath you can’t release.
Some words grow too heavy,
carried in pockets like loose change,
clinking with every step,
but never spent.
The screen glows,
soft as a confession,
and you wonder if they’d even hear it.
Delete.
Save.
Let it go.

scripts.topic: The Meaning We Choose to Find
scripts.script:
WE CARVE IT
from the cracks
where the sidewalk
used to be whole

we press our thumbs
into the soft clay
of what might have been

SOMEONE ELSE
would call it nothing

we call it
our own small religion

the way the rain
remembers the roof
long after the storm

and still
we kneel
there

scripts.topic: You Make the Hard Days Easier
scripts.script:
You adjust the pillow behind your back again.
The sheets still smell like detergent,
but underneath,
that faint trace of rain on skin.
I watch your fingers trace the edge of the blanket,
counting stitches like seconds.
Some days the weight feels impossible,
and then you hum,
just one low note,
and the room tilts back toward level.
Your laugh doesn’t fix anything,
but suddenly,
the cracks don’t feel so sharp.
send this to them.

How do you name the ache
that lives in your pockets,
weighing down every step
but never heavy enough to leave behind?
The coins you saved for a future,
now just jingling like hollow promises
with every move you make.
Some lessons aren’t taught,
they’re slipped into your palm
when you weren’t looking,
and suddenly,
all your change feels counterfeit.
Keep walking.
The weight’s yours to carry.

scripts.topic: The Small Things I Notice About You
scripts.script:
The way your fingers trace,
the rim of a coffee cup,
like it’s a secret map.
I memorize the pauses,
each hesitation,
a language only I can hear.
Some mornings,
your laugh arrives first,
then the words,
like sunlight through blinds,
unexpected,
but always right on time.

scripts.topic: Falling For Someone Who Does Not Know
scripts.script:
You trace the rim of your coffee cup.
Tiny cracks in the ceramic
remember every sip you didn’t take.
I watch the steam curl,
knowing it’s the only thing
you’ll let dissolve between us.
Your laugh lands like loose change
in a drawer I can’t open.
I count them anyway,
polish each one in my pocket.
The sugar in your tea never sweetens.
Send this to them.

I FOLD the map wrong
but the roads still lead me home
somewhere soft
where the radio hums old static
and no one asks why I took the long way
WHEN DID I STOP APOLOGIZING
for the shape of my hands
for the way my laughter spills
like loose change across a diner booth
I keep the quarters now
they jingle in my pocket
proof I was here
proof I stayed

scripts.topic: You Didn't Fix Me, You Stayed
scripts.script:
I stopped waiting for the glue to dry,
but you never peeled me off.
Some hands don’t mend cracks,
they just trace the lines,
slowly,
like reading a map of where I’ve been.
The table still wobbles,
and you,
you just put your palm down,
steady,
not to fix,
but to say,
I’m here through the shake.
Let them know.

scripts.topic: The Dreams Where They Come Back
scripts.script:
The dreams where they come back
are not ghosts,
just old film reels unspooling,
soft and flickering.
Some nights,
the projector hums louder,
and I reach through static
to touch a shoulder that isn’t there.
The screen goes white,
but the warmth lingers,
a half-remembered sunburn
on skin that forgot how to ache.

scripts.topic: What I Never Said Out Loud
scripts.script:
I kept the postcards
folded
in my back pocket
until the ink bled through,
WORDS LIKE
soft punches to the ribs
I never threw.
The clock on the wall
still ticks,
but slower now,
as if it remembers
what I never said
and waits.

The kettle whistles once,
then goes quiet.
I pour your cup anyway,
steam curling into nothing.
Some mornings,
the ghost of your tea bag
still stains the porcelain.
I drink it black now,
but the sugar bowl stays full,
just in case.
The spoons still clink,
not for stirring,
just to hear the sound.

scripts.topic: The Kind of Love That Doesn't Shout
scripts.script:
There's a love that hums,
not in anthems,
but in the way
kettles keep the water warm.
Some hands don't grip,
they cradle,
steady as a porch swing
that never asks the evening to hurry.
I won't call you home,
just leave the door unlatched,
the light inside
soft enough to guide,
but never blind.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Difference Between Living and Existing
scripts.script:
I stopped counting the hours
like loose change in my pocket.
The bedsheet still holds your shape,
but the mattress sighs when I turn.
WE ARE NOT THE SAME PERSON,
yet the room remembers the way you breathed.
I trace the cracks in the ceiling,
pretending they’re constellations of us.
Some nights,
the quiet hums your favorite song.
I press my palm to the wall,
and the plaster warms like skin.
send this to them.

You don’t belong here. Yet here you are.
Every room you enter
softens like butter in a warm pan.
I watch the edges melt,
noticing how the air thickens with your name.
No one else leaves fingerprints on my coffee mug,
still warm from the morning we shared.
The sink remembers your laugh,
the way it ripples through running water.
I keep forgetting to lock the door,
waiting for the sound of your keys.
Some homes aren’t built with bricks,
just the quiet certainty of your return.
Send this to them.

The spare key is still under the mat.
I step over it,
not out of spite,
but because the door
feels like a museum now.
Some rooms don’t fit you anymore,
the ceilings too low,
the walls whispering
of someone else’s life.
I leave the key where it lies,
just...
letting the rain rust it quiet.

scripts.topic: Why Peace Is Not the Absence of Storm
scripts.script:
Peace isn’t stillness.
Not really.
The river keeps moving,
carrying rocks,
shaping the shore,
but the water stays clear.
Some currents pull,
others let go,
and the depth never fights the flow.
You learn to float,
not because the storm stops,
but because you remember how.
The river knows your name.

scripts.topic: The Way You Look When You Do Not Know I Am Watching
scripts.script:
The way your thumbnail taps the table.
A tiny metronome of missing me.
I count the beats between thoughts,
wonder if you feel the rhythm too.
Your lips part,
soft as the first page of a book
no one’s opened yet.
The room holds its breath,
and I realize I’ve been reading you
like braille in the dim.
Every flicker is a word I know by heart.
send this to them.

Love was the fire,
but the house was already cold.
We kept feeding the flames,
just to watch them flicker,
not knowing the walls were hollow.
Some rooms can’t be warmed,
no matter how many logs you burn.
I traced the cracks in the ceiling,
counting the places light leaked in,
while you slept beneath them,
unaware the roof was gone.
The ash settled,
soft and final.
We mistook smoke for dawn.

Happiness isn’t what you think.
It doesn’t arrive.
It’s already here,
like the scent of rain on pavement
before the first drop falls.
You walk faster,
trying to outrun the storm
that’s never coming.
The truth is simpler:
You’ve been holding your breath
for the ocean to return,
but the tide was in your hands all along.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Text You Read But Did Not Reply To
scripts.script:
There’s a sting like static on the skin,
not from what you said,
but from what you didn’t.
The screen glows,
soft and accusatory,
like a held breath you can’t release.
Some words sit in the inbox,
warm and waiting,
not ghosts,
just quiet proof
you chose to stay silent.
I read it again,
and again,
until the letters blur into meaninglessness.

scripts.topic: What I Mean When I Say I Love You
scripts.script:
I stopped counting the ways.
Your laugh fits in my pocket,
soft as a crumpled bus ticket.
I press it to my ear sometimes,
and the city sounds different.
The way you stir sugar into coffee
is a secret language I’m learning.
Every swirl,
a constellation I can’t name yet.
I trace the shape of it
when you’re not looking.
Send this to them.

The little things you do,
are ripples in a pond,
not the stone,
just the way the water bends.
Some days I trace the circles,
with my fingertips,
not to change the shape,
but to remember it.
The hands that stir my coffee,
leave no mark,
just warmth that lingers,
after the cup is gone.

scripts.topic: How to Carry Loss Without Being Crushed
scripts.script:
It isn’t heavy. Just awkward.
Like a pocket full of river stones,
polished smooth by time,
but still taking up space.
You don’t drop them,
you just learn to walk differently.
The weight shifts,
settles into the hollows of your hips,
becomes part of the rhythm.
One day,
you’ll forget they’re there.
Until you reach in,
and your fingers remember.

scripts.topic: When Someone Chooses You Every Day
scripts.script:
I stopped counting the mornings.
Your name on my lips like a fresh cup.
Steam curls,
and I remember how you stir,
not for the sugar,
but for the way my fingers fit.
COFFEE GOES COLD,
but the habit stays.
You choose the chipped mug,
the one with my lipstick,
every single day.
I trace the cracks.
Send this to them.

YOU ASK HOW I AM
and I
smile
like a coin dropped in a fountain
wishing for one more second
before the question sinks.
The edges of my teeth
are worn smooth
from holding back
what I can’t afford to spend.
I COUNT THE CRACKS IN THE CEILING
when the house is quiet
and my face forgets
how to stay still.
EVERYONE SAYS I LOOK HAPPY
but happiness is just
what I borrowed
for the photograph.

scripts.topic: When You Cry in the Car Alone
scripts.script:
the radio hums
low
like a lullaby
for someone else
YOU PRESS your forehead
against the cool glass
watching
rain trace
streaks down the window
YOUR BREATH fogs
then clears
then fogs again
like a secret
you can't keep
but won't tell

scripts.topic: Why the Wound Is the Gift
scripts.script:
The salt shaker was still half-empty.
Your side of the table never moved,
but the cracks in the wood remember your elbows.
I trace them like constellations,
small galaxies of pressure and warmth.
THEY SAY EVERY BREAK LEAVES A MAP,
and yours leads straight to the kitchen,
where the kettle still hums your favorite note.
I drink it black now,
sugar bowl untouched,
but the bitterness tastes like a promise kept.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Way You Look at Me
scripts.script:
There’s a kind of hunger that doesn’t ask for food.
It watches,
quiet as the space between raindrops,
and suddenly,
you’re the only meal it wants.
Not your words,
not the way you laugh,
just the way your eyes rest on me
like a question I’ve already answered.
I don’t need you to speak,
I just need you to keep looking
like I’m the only thing worth seeing.

How do you measure a love
that’s not a scale
but a slow tide
pulling you farther each night
not from distance
but from knowing
the shore will always remember your shape
Some hearts don’t need to be held
to feel like home
and yours
was the first door I walked through
without knocking
I don’t miss you more than the sky
I just miss the way you fit inside it
send this to them.

There's a hunger that lingers
in the back of your throat,
not for food,
but for the way they used to swallow your words whole.
The bed still holds the shape of their absence,
a crater in the sheets,
and you press your palm into the hollow,
just to feel the weight of nothing.
Some nights,
you reheat the same cup of tea,
not because you want it,
but because the steam still looks like them,
vanishing into air.
You won't cry,
not today,
just let the kettle scream for you.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Small Things I Notice About You
scripts.script:
I never told you how your coffee cup
leaves a tiny crescent on the table.
The way you trace it with your thumb,
like a secret map only we can read.
Your laugh lands in the back of my throat,
soft as the first page of a book I’ve already memorized.
I notice the way your socks never match,
and how you hum when you think no one’s listening.
The quiet click of your pen,
pausing mid-sentence to look at me.
I carry these moments like loose change,
clinking in my pocket,
heavy with things I’ll never spend.

scripts.topic: The Beauty of an Unfinished Life
scripts.script:
A half-stitched quilt,
warm in the making,
not meant to be perfect.
Some threads fray,
others tangle,
but the pattern still breathes.
The edges curl,
unfinished,
and that’s where the story spills.
No final knot,
just the needle hovering,
waiting for the next color.
Some lives,
like this,
are beautiful in the becoming.

scripts.topic: You Make the Hard Days Easier
scripts.script:
You’re stirring honey into tea again.
The spoon clinks like a metronome,
steadying the room.
I watch the golden thread dissolve,
sweetness spreading without sound.
You don’t ask if I’m tired.
You just slide the mug closer,
warmth bleeding through the ceramic.
The clock ticks,
but time feels softer here.
Like the way your voice wraps around my edges,
rounding the sharp corners.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: Falling For Someone Who Does Not Know
scripts.script:
You’re tracing circles on your knee
like a map of somewhere you’ve never been
but the ink keeps bleeding through the paper
softly
like a pulse you can’t control
Some nights you swear they hear it
the way a seashell remembers the ocean
not in words
just a hum beneath their skin
You won’t say it aloud
not even in the shower where the tiles echo back
just let the water run colder
and pretend it’s the truth.

Friendships don’t always end with a fight.
They unravel like a sweater left in a drawer,
thread by thread,
you don’t notice
until the sleeves are too short,
the neckline stretched.
Some bonds are just wool,
soft but not meant to last.
I kept the pieces anyway,
folded neatly in my chest,
not as a scar,
but as a reminder—
some warmth was real.
Let them go.

4:17 a.m. The kettle hums,
still warm from yesterday.
You stir honey,
but the spoon tastes like someone else’s habit.
The mug remembers your grip,
not the way your fingers hesitate now.
Somewhere between the sugar and the steam,
you stopped being the one who chose.
The tea goes cold,
and you drink it anyway.
not because you want it,
but because it’s already poured.

scripts.topic: What Truly Matters in the End
scripts.script:
You're tracing circles on the steamy bathroom mirror.
The fog remembers your fingers.
Five little orbits,
like planets that never collide.
BUT THE WATER keeps running,
cooling your wrists,
while the shower curtain whispers your name.
I THINK WHAT TRULY MATTERS
is the way you still leave the cap off the toothpaste,
knowing I'll put it back,
and how that tiny rebellion feels like trust.
The mirror clears.
Send this to them.

I leave the porch light cold
so you won’t know
I’m watching
from the kitchen window
YOU TURN YOUR HEAD
just once
like you heard me think your name
THE CURTAIN FALLS
back into place
still warm
from my fingers
I pretend not to notice

You don’t need stillness.
Not the kind you chase.
just the quiet hum
of your own pulse,
steady as a metronome
forgotten in a drawer.
Some days it skips,
not from fear,
but because it’s learning
the rhythm of your breath.
The room doesn’t change.
You do.
let it.

The first time I knew,
I was a child on a train,
watching fields blur into rivers,
not from speed,
but from the way my eyelids fluttered.
Somewhere between awake and dreaming,
the world tilted,
and I held my breath,
not to stop time,
but to feel it slide,
like a coin in my pocket,
warm and heavy,
with the weight of something real.

scripts.topic: The Meaning We Choose to Find
scripts.script:
How do you explain the weight of a pocket,
not from coins or keys,
but from the habit of reaching,
just to feel something there.
Some days,
you fill it with pebbles,
small and smooth,
carrying them like promises,
not because they mean anything,
but because you chose to believe,
they might.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: What You Cannot Change and What You Can
scripts.script:
There's a quiet like static in the ears
not silence
just the hum of what won't leave
The clock hands you can't slow
but the way you trace their path
changes everything
Some days you're the river
carving stone
not by force
but by returning
I can't stop the rain
but I can choose which puddles to step in
The weight stays
but my shoulders remember how to rise
not for them
just for the next breath
and the next
send this to the one who thinks they're stuck.

You’re unfolding the blanket again
like it’s a map to somewhere safe
Some nights
it’s the only border I trust
between my skin and the world
The stitches hold
but the threads remember your weight
Not as proof
just as warmth that lingers
like a second heartbeat
I don’t need to name it
to know it’s home
let them know.

scripts.topic: The One I Think About First
scripts.script:
There's a hunger that only remembers your laugh.
The way it curled around my ribs,
soft as a cat
warming the space between my lungs.
I still reach for it
when the fridge hums empty at 2 a.m.
Not for food,
but the memory of being fed.
Your voice on the phone
was always the first bite.
I chew on it now,
slowly,
like a secret I can't swallow.

scripts.topic: What I Never Said Out Loud
scripts.script:
I kept the last train ticket
stuck
between pages
like a secret I could still return to
YOU NEVER ASKED
why my coat pockets smelled of rain
and old paper
I never told you
about the bench
where I counted minutes
until the ache felt like a second skin
WE BOTH KNEW
but we
let the words
fade
into the hum of streetlamps

scripts.topic: When You Have to Pretend You Are Fine at Work
scripts.script:
The coffee cup stays full all morning.
Nobody notices the steam has stopped rising.
You learn to smile
with your teeth only,
just...
a quiet click of bone against bone.
Some days the mask is porcelain,
smooth and cold to the touch,
but...
the cracks still whisper your real name.
The clock ticks,
and...
you count the seconds between breaths,
not...
to measure time,
but...
to remember you’re still alive.
Send this to them.

The draft folder smells like old paperbacks.
I trace the words
like fingerprints on a fogged mirror.
They stay,
even when the screen goes black.
Every unsent message
is a postcard I licked shut.
No stamp,
no return address,
just the quiet ache of wanting to say.
The cursor blinks,
patient as a cat at midnight.
I delete the whole thing.
Send this to them.

How do you measure the weight of a habit,
not the grand gestures,
but the way your fingers
remember to leave the porch light on,
even when I’m already home.
Some nights,
I trace the edges of your routine
like a map I’ve memorized,
and I wonder if you know
that every small kindness
is a door left unlocked for me.
I won’t ask you to stay,
just let me keep the key.

Your mouth curves like a secret,
folded
into itself.
A smile,
but the corners
are tired.
I know
you’re not lying,
just
saving the truth
for someone
who won’t flinch.

scripts.topic: The Difference Between Living and Existing
scripts.script:
Living is a handprint on wet glass.
Existing is the streak that dries.
Some fingers press deep,
others barely touch,
but both leave marks.
The rain remembers,
even when the pane forgets.
I trace the shapes
not to bring them back,
just to prove they were real.
The difference is the ache,
not the absence.

scripts.topic: Missing a Person Who Is Still Here
scripts.script:
It’s not absence. It’s the shape of them,
still folded in your pocket like a ticket
never used.
You reach,
but the seat beside you stays warm from your own body.
Some mornings,
you swear you hear the coffee mug clink,
the one they always chose,
but the kitchen hums empty,
and the sugar bowl remembers their fingerprints.
You don’t ache.
You just keep setting the table for two.
Let them know.

4:17 a.m. The kettle hums,
just below a whistle.
You pour,
not for taste,
but for the ritual of choosing yourself.
The steam rises,
soft against your wrists,
like a reminder:
Some thresholds aren’t crossed,
they’re quietly claimed.
Your name on the mug,
not theirs,
not anyone’s.
The quiet revolution begins here.

How do you name the space
between goodbye and forever?
The chair stays pulled up to the table,
the coffee cup never moved.
Some mornings,
the bed still holds your shape
like a promise half-kept.
I don’t ask for rain,
but the umbrella leans
against the same wall,
waiting for a storm that won’t come.
Not absence,
just quiet devotion.
Let them know.

scripts.topic: Being Strong for Everyone While Breaking Inside
scripts.script:
You’re not falling apart. Just the stitches.
Every smile you sew
pulls tighter,
but the thread is your own ribs.
PEOPLE praise the steady hands,
the way you mend their cracks without flinching.
No one notices the spool running empty.
The last knot slips loose,
and suddenly,
you’re the one unraveling.
Hold the needle anyway.
Send this to them.

You're tracing the cracks in the sidewalk again.
The city hums,
but you hear the maple syrup
slowly thickening in the pan.
TRUST isn't a straight line—
it's the way your grandmother's hands
knew when the sugar was ready,
just by the slow drip,
the way it clung to the spoon.
You don't have to see the whole path,
just the next sweet spill.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: When You Cry in the Car Alone
scripts.script:
The steering wheel remembers every tear,
not as salt,
but as wax from a candle,
dripping slow,
melting the dashboard into a shrine.
Some nights,
the radio plays static hymns,
and the headlights carve tunnels
through fog,
just wide enough
for one confession,
then the road swallows it whole.

I kept the receipts
folded small
in my back pocket
like they’d prove something.
YOU THREW OUT THE RULES
without telling me
the game changed.
Now the coffee tastes different
same cup
same spoon
same empty chair.
I still reach
for the sugar
twice.

There's a patience that doesn't wait,
just lingers like a half-drawn bath,
the water still warm,
but no one steps in.
Some doors don't need pushing,
they breathe open when you're not looking,
and the air that rushes through
is softer than you remember.
I won't force the lock,
just leave my palm against the wood,
quiet,
knowing some things arrive
when you stop reaching.

YOU LEAVE your coffee cup
on my counter
still warm
I press my palm to the ceramic
like a promise
no one else will see
THE HOUSE remembers
every creak of your steps
even when you’re not here
I count them
like rosary beads
soft
soft
softer

There's a quiet like fresh snowfall,
unmarked,
soft enough to swallow sound.
Your name on my lips
melts before it reaches air.
I trace the shape of you
in the steam on my coffee cup,
watch it disappear
with every slow sip.
The room remembers your weight,
the couch still dips where you sat.
I don't need to speak it
to keep you here.

scripts.topic: When You Love Someone Who Is Leaving
scripts.script:
You don’t leave. Not really.
The suitcase sits half-packed,
zipper teeth still unclenched.
I count the hours like loose change,
spilling between couch cushions.
Your voice lingers in the hallway,
soft as the scent of rain on pavement.
I press my palm to the empty space,
where your laughter used to live.
The apartment breathes,
but it’s learning to exhale without you.
I’ll keep the door unlocked.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: Being Tired in Ways Sleep Cannot Fix
scripts.script:
I WAKE
like a clock
hands stuck
on the same minute
my bones hum
an old ache
not pain
just memory
I DRINK COFFEE
that tastes like yesterday
and the day before
the steam curls
into shapes I almost recognize
YOU ASK IF I'M OKAY
I nod
too tired to lie
properly

scripts.topic: When You Love Someone You Cannot Have
scripts.script:
I stopped counting the cracks in the ceiling.
Every night they map your name,
soft as chalk on pavement.
The bed holds the shape of someone else,
but the sheets still smell like rain on warm skin.
I trace the outline,
slowly,
like a child learning braille.
The pillow knows,
and it won’t tell.
I press my ear to it,
listening for the echo of your breath.
This stays between us.

scripts.topic: The Beauty of an Unfinished Life
scripts.script:
There’s a quiet hunger
that doesn’t ask for much,
just the space
to keep becoming.
The pages stay open,
not out of fear,
but because the story
still hums inside you.
Some chapters
are just sketches,
soft pencil lines
waiting to be traced.
And that’s the beauty—
you’re not done yet.

scripts.topic: What Truly Matters in the End
scripts.script:
The way your laugh
fills a room
before you do
REMEMBER THAT
not the awards
not the titles
just the sound
of someone
who still knows
how to be happy
for no reason
at all.

The bathroom door stays unlocked.
Not for safety,
for the way your fingers brush mine
when you reach for the towel I just used.
WARMTH LINGERS like steam on glass,
condensing into something we don’t name.
The sink holds two toothbrushes,
but only one cup.
I drink from yours anyway.
BEDSHEETS still smell like the detergent
you chose last summer,
when you decided we were worth
softening for.
send this to them.

I stopped counting the cracks in my teacup.
The porcelain remembers every sip we shared,
but the tea leaves settle,
unreadable now.
I trace the hairline fractures,
wondering when the whole thing will shatter.
Some breaks don’t make a sound.
The kettle still hums,
but the melody’s off-key,
and I pour the water anyway.
The cup holds,
for now.
send this to them.

The river forgets its name every morning,
curling into itself like a question mark,
just to remember it again by noon.
Somewhere between the bend and the bank,
you learn to float,
not because the water holds you,
but because you stop fighting the current.
The reeds don’t apologize for swaying,
they simply lean,
and in that lean,
you find the shape of peace,
unclenched, unhurried,
breathing in time with the tide.

scripts.topic: The Small Things I Notice About You
scripts.script:
The way your fingers trace,
the rim of a coffee cup,
like you’re reading braille
in the steam.

Some mornings,
you hum off-key,
just one note,
repeated,
as if testing the air.

I notice the way,
your eyelashes catch,
the last of the rain,
and hold it,
until the sun dries them,
without you ever knowing.

You don’t break. Not like glass.
just like river stones,
polished
by everything you carry.
The weight doesn’t disappear,
it becomes the shape of you.
Some days,
you wake up smoother,
less jagged,
but heavier in ways no one sees.
The cracks still catch the rain,
and that’s how you learn to hold water.
You were never meant to stay sharp.

The little things you do,
like folding corners of pages
to save my place.
Not with bookmarks,
just creases,
tiny white flags
I find weeks later.
Some days,
I unfold them slowly,
pretending the paper remembers
your touch.

scripts.topic: What You Cannot Change and What You Can
scripts.script:
You’re tracing the cracks in the porcelain cup.
The tea’s gone cold hours ago.
Some stains never wash out,
but the way you hold it—
That’s yours to shape.
Fingers curve like a question,
soft against the chipped edge.
The past is just a story,
and stories can be rewritten.
You choose the ending,
even if the beginning stays.
Send this to them.

YOU TAUGHT ME
how to hold
what I couldn’t keep
like a handful of rain
slipping through my fingers
I learned
to open my palm
and watch it fall
not with a splash
but a whisper
as if the earth
had been waiting
all along

scripts.topic: When Love Feels Like Coming Home
scripts.script:
You're tracing circles on my kitchen table.
The way your fingers remember the grain.
Not like wood,
like the back of my neck.
I didn't know I had a shape
until you learned it by heart.
The kettle hums,
but it's just us,
just the steam rising between our words.
Some doors don't creak when they open.
They sigh,
and suddenly,
you're home.

The river heron knows.
release the fish
back to the current.
no ceremony
no backward glance.

you clutch the memory
like a pebble in your palm
smooth and warm
but the water moves on.

your hands were never meant
to hold the tide.

Hafiz whispered
beneath the laughter
lies a room
no guest has entered.
its walls are woven
from threads of questions
you never voiced.
what if the smile
is just the door
left ajar
so someone
might step inside.

scripts.topic: When You Have to Pretend You Are Fine at Work
scripts.script:
You’re smiling through the spreadsheet,
calm as a still lake.
The numbers blur,
but your teeth stay bright,
polished like the office floors.
Some days,
the weight isn’t sadness—
it’s the effort of holding still,
like a glass filled to the rim,
not spilling,
not even trembling.
The clock ticks,
and you remember:
even statues breathe.
visual_keywords: empty conference room with fluorescent lights

scripts.script:
I stopped counting the cracks in the sidewalk
when I realized
each one was a memory
trying to escape
The pavement remembers
but I don’t have to
Some days I walk barefoot
just to feel the imperfections
They sting for a second
then fade into warmth
I won’t smooth them over
let them stay uneven
let them tell their story.

Some loves are like rivers,
flowing past your doorstep,
not asking to be invited in,
just humming,
a low, constant tune.
I built a dock from memory,
planks warped with time,
where I sit,
feet dangling,
watching your reflection ripple,
never reaching for the oars.

A door left ajar
just enough
to let the smell of toast in
and the sound of someone humming off-key
SOMEWHERE IN THE HOUSE
not for you
not for anyone
just the way morning spills
into the hallway
like a cat stretching
YOU STEP IN
and the floorboards remember your weight
like an old joke
no one else gets.

scripts.topic: The Kind of Love That Doesn't Shout
scripts.script:
Hafiz whispered
love that doesn’t shout
leaves room for the moon
to rise in your chest.

It’s the kind that lingers
like salt on your skin
after the ocean’s gone,
not the wave that crashes
but the tide that stays.

You’ll find it in the way
the earth holds a footprint
long after the foot’s walked on.

4:17 a.m. The kettle hums,
just once,
then goes quiet.
Your shoulders remember the weight
before your hands do.
Some people leave,
but the shape of them lingers
like a coat you keep hanging by the door,
not for them,
for the version of you that still believes
in holding space.
The steam rises,
and you let it.

A single drop in the river,
shifted the current.
I didn’t notice at first,
just the way the water bent,
curving around my ankles like a secret.
The banks remembered,
softened under the weight of what you carried.
Some days,
even the stones leaned in,
whispering your name to the reeds.
And I,
I became the shore,
holding the shape of the wave.

scripts.topic: Missing Someone Who Is Right There
scripts.script:
The air between us hums,
like a fridge left open.
I hear the cold,
but I don’t reach to close it.
Some nights,
the door stays ajar,
letting out all the warmth.
I tell myself,
it’s just the way things are.
The hum doesn’t stop,
just fades into the background,
like your voice,
right there,
but not for me.

We were two rivers,
flowing side by side,
carving the same earth,
until the map changed.
Somewhere,
a dam was built,
not out of anger,
just time,
and the water found another way.
I still taste your salt,
in the rain that falls,
here,
where the shore remembers.

scripts.topic: The Meaning We Choose to Find
scripts.script:
There's a quiet ache
that lives between the ribs
not hunger
not sorrow
just the space where meaning used to be.
Some days it hums like an empty fridge
soft and persistent
reminding you nothing's there.
But you open it anyway
hoping the light will find something new
and it does—
not food
not answers
just the courage to close the door.

scripts.topic: The One I Think About First
scripts.script:
You tap your fingers against the mug.
Steam curls like a question you won’t ask.
The kitchen clock hums,
but its hands move too slow for your pulse.
I know the shape of your hesitation—
that half-second pause before you smile.
It fits inside my ribcage like a missing puzzle piece.
Your name tastes like the first sip of coffee,
bitter then sweet then necessary.
I don’t think about you first.
I think about the way you laugh at nothing,
how your shoulders relax when you’re truly listening,
and then I realize I’m already there.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: When Someone Chooses You Every Day
scripts.script:
I stopped counting the mornings
when your keys hit the counter first
not because I didn’t notice
but because I started breathing again
The way you reach for my coffee mug
like it’s the only thing you’ve ever held steady
Some days I forget to be amazed
and then you choose me anyway
just by walking in
with the same quiet certainty
you’ve carried for years
I won’t ask why
let them know.

Hafiz whispered
like sand through fingers
love slips
not from carelessness
but the tide itself.

you held the hourglass
watched each grain fall
knowing it was never yours to keep.

the shore remembers every footprint
but the ocean never does.

I stopped counting the cracks in the sidewalk.
Every one led back to you,
like a river that memorized its own bed.
The way your fingers brushed my sleeve—
a single thread pulled loose from the whole world.
I tried to weave it back,
but the needle was your voice,
the thread was always yours.
Now the sidewalk remembers for me.
It hums your name under every step,
and I pretend not to notice.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Difference Between Living and Existing
scripts.script:
I stopped counting the cracks in the ceiling
three years ago
but the plaster still remembers my weight
Every night I press my palm to the wall
just to feel something solid
Some people float through rooms like helium
never touching the floor
I built a house out of paper clips
bent them into tiny cages
and called it home
The difference isn’t breath
it’s the way your ribs expand
when someone says your name
I’ll leave the door unlocked.

scripts.topic: When You Miss Someone You Should Not
scripts.script:
Some nights are a locked drawer,
unopened for years.
I trace the brass handle,
warm from my palm,
but never lift the latch.
The hinge still creaks in memory,
soft as a secret told once,
then swallowed whole.
Your voice lingers in the grooves,
like fingerprints on old vinyl,
playing only when I’m not listening.
I let it skip,
let it fade,
pretend I don’t know the words.

scripts.topic: The Way You Look at Me
scripts.script:
The way your eyelashes brush your cheek.
I count them like rosary beads,
soft prayers I whisper only to myself.
Your gaze lingers,
and suddenly the room tilts,
the floor becomes a raft on still water.
I forget how to breathe properly,
my ribs ache with something sweeter than oxygen.
You don’t even have to speak,
just let your pupils widen like doorways.
I step inside without hesitation.
Send this to them.

The voicemail still saves your name in blue.
I trace the letters like braille,
but the screen stays cold.
EVERY NIGHT I rehearse the words,
soft enough to slip through cracks in doors.
You never knock back.
The charger cord dangles,
still plugged into the wall,
waiting for a phone that never rings.
I count the minutes like loose change,
saving them for a call that won’t come.
The bedsheets remember your shape.
I don’t.
send this to them.

There's a kind of peace
that tastes like salt on sunburned skin,
not healing,
just existing.
The ocean doesn't apologize
for how far it reaches,
and neither do I anymore.
Some waves were meant to crash,
others to carry you home.
I stopped fighting the current,
let the tide decide,
and found my feet
where the water meets the shore.

I trace your name
in the steam
on bathroom mirrors
before it fades.
We sit too close
on the couch
pretending not to notice
how the air hums
when our shoulders touch.
YOU NEVER LOOK
but your fingers
still find mine
in the popcorn bowl.

I stopped counting the steps,
but the staircase still creaks in my chest.
Some nights,
the weight of an empty mailbox
feels like a gravestone,
not for what died,
but for what never arrived.
The letters I wrote in my head,
folded into pockets,
never sent,
just yellowing with time.
I won’t dig them up.
Let the ink bleed into dust.

I stopped counting the days
since happiness became a room
I keep walking past.
The door stays ajar,
yellow like a forgotten lemon
on the kitchen counter.
I touch the peel,
and the scent clings to my fingers—
not sweet,
but proof it was real once.
The truth is simple:
I left the light on inside.
send this to them.

I never told you about the jar.
On my desk,
a single firefly blinks.
Its glow is the size of your smallest freckle,
steady,
like the way you hum off-key in empty rooms.
I unscrew the lid,
watch the light escape,
but it never reaches your window.
The jar stays,
collecting dust,
and your name in Morse code.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Way You Look When You Do Not Know I Am Watching
scripts.script:
The way your fingers trace the rim,
unaware,
of porcelain so thin it hums.
A cup left trembling,
just for the steam to rise,
like a thought you almost caught.
Some mornings,
you are the tide,
pulling back without a sound,
leaving only salt,
and the shape of where you were.

scripts.topic: Why the Wound Is the Gift
scripts.script:
The scar remembers
what the skin forgot.
It hums
low in the rain,
a quiet Morse
only your bones
can still translate.
You press your thumb
to the ridge,
and the past
answers—
warmer than you are.
visual_keywords: steam rising from a chipped mug

scripts.topic: Being Tired in Ways Sleep Cannot Fix
scripts.script:
The body keeps score,
even when the mind forgets.
Every step feels like wading through wet cement,
not heavy,
just slow.
The clock ticks,
but the hands don’t move forward,
they just circle the same empty hour.
Some days,
you’re not tired,
you’re just out of reasons to stand.
The bed calls,
but the sheets feel like strangers,
and sleep is just another place you don’t belong.
You close your eyes,
and the weight stays.

scripts.topic: When You Love Someone Who Is Leaving
scripts.script:
You packed your bag in silence
I watched
the way your fingers
traced the zipper’s teeth
like counting down seconds
to something neither of us named.
YOUR KEYS jingled once on the counter
still warm from my palm
the door clicked
not a slam
just a breath held too long
then the hallway swallowed you
whole.

You tuck your lips behind your teeth.
A fortress no one taught you how to leave.
The corners rise like a secret unlocked,
but the key is already lost.
Even the air knows,
presses closer,
asks what you’re guarding.
Your eyes flicker—
just for a second—
then the gates seal again.
I memorize the shape of it anyway.
send this to them.

You’re holding the door half-open.
The hinge creaks,
just enough to wake the house.
Some nights
are made for tiptoeing,
but your heart is a drum
no carpet can muffle.
You count the cracks in the ceiling,
three,
seven,
twelve—
and the courage isn’t in leaving,
it’s in the way your fingers
remember how to turn a key.

scripts.topic: When Someone Chooses You Every Day
scripts.script:
YOU WAKE UP FIRST
and leave the coffee brewing
while I pretend to sleep.

Every morning
the same creak of the floorboard
like a promise
kept in secret code.

I COUNT the seconds
between your steps
and the clink of the mug
on my nightstand.

SOMEHOW
this quiet ritual
feels heavier
than any vow.

scripts.topic: The Beauty of an Unfinished Life
scripts.script:
There's a hunger that doesn't ask for food,
just the space to keep going.
The road still hums,
unpaved,
and your shoes are dust,
not defeat.
Some days,
you're the unfinished sentence,
not the period.
The hands that built you
are still trembling,
but the blueprint stays open.
Let them wonder.

scripts.topic: When Love Feels Like Coming Home
scripts.script:
You leave your key in the lock
again
like it belongs there
like the door still knows your shape
OPENING
without a sound
and I’m not surprised
just softer
when your hands find my waist
in the hallway
where the floorboards creak
under the weight of us
coming home

You're stirring honey into tea too fast.
The spoon clinks like a warning.
Some things dissolve better
if you let them sit.
The cup knows.
It doesn't rush the sweetness.
You watch the golden spiral
slowly unravel,
remembering how tides
never force the shore.
The ocean just loves it,
and the waves keep coming.
send this to them.

Some loves are ink underwater,
bleeding slow,
no one sees the sentence form.
I trace your name
on fogged glass,
till the letters drip,
vanish before they dry.
The heart’s a clock with hands,
but no face,
ticking loudest when you’re gone.
I set the alarm,
let it ring,
just to feel you near.

I stopped collecting the train tickets.
They pile like autumn leaves in my drawer,
crisp edges,
unread destinations.
You used to press them into my palm,
warm from your pocket.
Now the conductor just nods,
and the seats beside me stay empty.
I still trace the ink,
but the numbers blur,
like cities I’ll never reach.
send this to them.

The last text you sent yourself still glows,
unopened.
Your name in the search bar,
like a secret you keep from everyone.
Fingers hover,
but the send button stays cold.
SOME DAYS,
you forget you’re the only one reading.
The drafts folder,
full of letters no one else will ever need.
You sign them,
and the ink dries into belonging.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: When You Outgrow Your Best Friend
scripts.script:
We were two rivers,
carving the same valley,
side by side.
Then the land tilted,
and I flowed faster,
leaving you in the shallows.
Some nights,
I still hear the current,
but it’s not your voice anymore.
The banks we shaped,
now strangers,
holding only memories of rain.

This river doesn’t apologize,
carving canyons without asking.
Some mornings,
the bed is still warm,
but the body that shaped it
has slipped into the next hour.
I trace the dent,
not to keep,
just to remember
how soft the weight was,
how fleeting.

scripts.topic: The Small Things I Notice About You
scripts.script:
You’re not quiet. Not really.
The way you stir your coffee—
three slow circles,
the spoon clicks like a secret.
I count the freckles on your wrist,
map them like constellations
no one else has named.
Your laugh arrives before you do,
soft as the crease behind your ear
where I press my lips
and pretend it’s a question.
Send this to them.

You swallow the crack in your voice
like stale bread
no one hears it stick
YOU KEEP
walking
past the mirror that knows
your face better than you do
it blinks
slow
and you pretend
not to notice
that it’s crying too

scripts.topic: When You Make Ordinary Moments Feel Different
scripts.script:
You stir your coffee
slow
like it’s the first time
you’ve ever held a spoon
and the steam
curls into shapes
that only you can name.
I watch
the way your lashes brush the rim
of the cup
and suddenly
the kitchen
is a place I’ve never been
but always known.
The clock still ticks
but the seconds
feel like secrets
we’re keeping
from the rest of the world.

scripts.topic: When You Miss Someone You Should Not
scripts.script:
The voicemail still saves your last laugh.
I press play,
but the speaker only hums static.
YOUR NAME glows on the screen,
bright as a streetlamp I walk under,
trying to remember the way home.
The couch cushions hold your shape,
like water remembering a dropped stone.
I trace the dip with my palm,
wondering if you feel this too.
The kettle whistles,
but the tea tastes like absence.
Send this to them.

How do you measure the weight of a glance,
that lingers just a second too long,
but warms the room like a struck match?
The way your fingers remember,
to leave the last bite on my plate,
not out of pity,
but because you know I’ll reach for it,
and you want to be the one who gives.
Some habits don’t need words,
they live in the crease of your sleeves,
where you tuck away kindness,
like loose change for someone else’s day.
I keep finding it,
long after you’ve gone.

You’re stirring honey into tea.
The spoon swirls like a slow tide.
I watch your wrist,
the way it curves—
soft as the first page of a book
no one else has opened.
The steam rises,
carrying the scent of something sweet
I can’t name but recognize.
This is how you hold me:
not in your arms,
in the quiet hum of the kitchen.
Send this to them.

I stopped counting the cracks in my favorite mug.
Every chip holds a morning I thought I couldn’t face,
but the porcelain never breaks.
It just learns to hold more.
The tea leaves settle differently now,
swirling into shapes that look like answers.
I trace the fractures with my thumb,
and the warmth stays longer than it used to.
Some things don’t heal,
they just become the hands that pour.
Send this to them.

scripts.topic: The Exhaustion of Pretending to Be Okay
scripts.script:
The toaster still pops at 7:03.
You butter both slices.
A habit,
not hunger.
The kitchen clock hums,
its hands stuck in yesterday’s routine.
You count the crumbs,
pretend they’re reasons.
The butter melts,
but the ache doesn’t.
You eat standing up.
The plate stays warm.
send this to them.

2:17 a.m. The kettle hums,
but the water never boils.
I trace the cracks in your mug,
slowly,
like reading old texts at midnight.
The porcelain remembers your lips,
but I’m learning to drink alone.
Some lessons taste like lukewarm tea—
bitter,
and best forgotten.
I won’t pour it out,
just let the steam fade,
unseen.

scripts.topic: The Difference Between Living and Existing
scripts.script:
Living is a kitchen table
set for one more
than you expected.
EXISTING
is eating cereal
from the box
standing over the sink.
Living tastes like
salt on buttered toast
when someone else made it.
EXISTING
is the fridge humming
its lonely tune
while you forget what you came for.
Living
is the difference
between a door left open
and one you walk past
without looking back.

scripts.topic: Grieving Someone Who Did Not Die
scripts.script:
Tuesday afternoon. The kettle hums,
but the cup stays empty.
I trace the cracks in the table,
not where you sat,
just where the wood remembers your weight.
Some mornings,
I still set two spoons,
and watch the second one,
slowly,
learn to stay still.
The house breathes,
but the air tastes different now.
I won’t erase your voice,
just the habit of waiting.

The clock hands froze mid-air.
You didn’t notice,
just kept counting ticks.
Somewhere a door,
hinges rusted from disuse,
creaked open on its own.
No one entered.
The air smelled like old coins,
heavy in your palm.
You let them slip,
not with a clatter,
but a whisper of surrender.

scripts.topic: When No One Asks If You Are Okay
scripts.script:
There’s a hunger no one names,
not the kind that gnaws your ribs,
just a slow, quiet ache
that settles in the throat.
The kind that watches plates pass,
but never gets a seat.
Some people notice the empty chair,
but no one asks why it’s cold.
You learn to swallow the question whole,
chew on silence instead.
It tastes like nothing,
and nothing is what they expect.
Let them.

scripts.topic: You Make the Hard Days Easier
scripts.script:
You’re stirring honey into tea again.
The spoon clinks against porcelain,
steady.
Not too fast,
not too slow—
like the way you hum when the radio statics.
I watch the amber swirl,
thick and golden,
until the whole cup tastes like home.
Some days the world is a locked door.
You jiggle the handle,
say *try this*—
and suddenly,
it opens.

4:17 a.m. The radiator hums low.
Your thumb lingers,
just above the screen,
not scrolling,
waiting for the glow to feel like home.
Some nights,
you swear the pixels rearrange themselves,
forming words you almost recognize.
The last message still sits,
unanswered,
but the timestamp updates,
quietly,
like a pulse you can’t ignore.
Let them know.

A Japanese gardener whispered
keep the stone
that once blocked your path
it remembers the shape of your foot
long after the rain washes the mud away.

You save their number
not for the calls you’ll make
but for the weight of what wasn’t said
still humming in the spaces between digits.

The phone sleeps in your pocket
a quiet archive of what could have been
and what still might.
Let it rest there
like a pebble in a stream
neither held too tight
nor thrown away.

You don’t trust the path. Not yet.
A compass in your palm
spins like a drunk dancer.
The trail isn’t straight,
just a series of small yeses.
Your feet remember the way
before your mind does.
That first step wasn’t courage,
only curiosity wearing your shoes.
The river doesn’t ask permission,
and neither should you.
This isn’t surrender.
It’s learning to float.
send this to them.

scripts.topic: Being Tired in Ways Sleep Cannot Fix
scripts.script:
You’re stirring your coffee,
the spoon scraping,
but the sugar won’t dissolve.
Your bones ache,
not from the climb,
but the weight of standing still.
Some days,
the bed is a raft,
and you’re already drowning.
The clock hums,
a lullaby for the exhausted,
but your eyes stay open,
searching for a shore
no tide can bring.

scripts.topic: How to Carry Loss Without Being Crushed
scripts.script:
There's a weight that settles like dust
on the shelves of your ribs.
Not heavy enough to break,
just enough to remind you
every time you breathe.
The jars stay upright,
but the labels blur.
You learn to carry them
one at a time,
not as treasures,
not as burdens—
just things that belong.
send this to them.

Sunday evening. The week hasn’t started.
The kettle hums,
but the steam curls
like fingers tracing old promises.
I sit
and let the quiet settle,
not to escape,
but to remember
what my pulse sounds like
when no one’s listening.
The strength isn’t in moving—
it’s in knowing
you can stay.

scripts.topic: You Didn't Fix Me, You Stayed
scripts.script:
The teacup still has your lipstick mark.
I never washed it off.
A tiny crescent moon,
soft pink,
perched on porcelain like a secret.
You didn’t fix the crack,
